section.para: While purely self-motivated creation can exist outside the service paradigm, the hypothesis that "all externally directed IP creation is an act of service" holds true under rigorous legal and economic scrutiny. The act of external direction—whether through a formal contract, an algorithmic nudge, or a patron's request—is legally interpreted as an exercise of control. When this control is coupled with any form of remuneration, it establishes a service relationship.

In jurisdictions like California, this relationship is presumptively one of employment, with transformative consequences for IP ownership and financial liability. The law looks past the labels of "user," "patron," or "contributor" to the functional reality of the exchange. In that reality, the creator who follows an external party's direction is providing a service, and the resulting IP is the deliverable of that service.
